{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Komushi Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:与謝野町温江 子虫神社.jpg|300px]] |location = [[Kyoto Prefecture]], [[Yosa District]], [[Yosano Town]], Atsue, Omoriya 103 |coordinates = {{ill|Wikicoordinates|en|ウィキ座標2段度分秒}}|35|30|02.7|N|135|06|31.8|E|scale:25000 |deity = {{ill|Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona}} |rank = {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大}}), Former Village Shrine |founded = Unknown |main_hall = {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} |alias = |festival = Last Sunday in April |ceremonies = Kayadani Festival (last Saturday and Sunday in April) }}
'''Komushi Shrine''' ({{ill|Komushi Shrine|ja|小虫神社}}) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Atsue]], [[Yosano Town]], [[Yosa District]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]]. It is a designated {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大}}) and ranked as a [[Village Shrine]] under the [[Modern typeing System]]. The shrine is situated on an eastern plateau of the Kayadani area along the midstream of the {{ill|Nodagawa River (Kyoto)|ja|野田川}}, at the western base of the Ōeyama mountain range. Kayadani is known for its concentration of ancient Kofuns and archaeological sites, such as the nationally designated [[Hiruko-yama Kofun]] and the [[Sakuyama Kofun (Yosano Town)|Sakuyama Kofun]], making it a key area in the ancient [[Tango Province]].
== Deities == * {{ill|Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona}} (The shrine records from 1883 note an alternate tradition enshrining "Wakayama-kui no Mikoto." <ref>Included in Volume 1 of the "Kayachō History: Materials Edition."</ref>) * {{ill|Kagutsuchi no Mikoto|ja|火産霊命|1=Kagu-tsuchi}} (from Hirogaki Shrine, merged in 1877) * {{ill|Ōyamatsumi no Mikoto|ja|大山祇命|1=Ōyamatsumi}} (from Kijimi Shrine, merged simultaneously with Hirogaki Shrine)

According to legend, {{ill|Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona}} cured the poisoned Numagawa-hime, who had fallen ill due to the malice of a demon named Tsuchi-oni. This act gave rise to the worship of "Ōmushi" and "Komushi" as protector deities.<ref name="InvestigationReport">"Shikinai Shrine Investigation Report."</ref>
Additionally, the site is associated with the military expeditions of {{ill|Prince Maroko|ja|麻呂子親王}}, a son of {{ill|Emperor Yōmei|ja|用明天皇}}, who is said to have prayed here before subduing rebellious forces in Ōeyama.

A remnant of the former site, known as "Mitarashi Pond," is said to exist.</ref>
In 1873, the shrine was classified as a [[Village Shrine]] under [[Toyooka Prefecture]]. The current {{ill|Main Hall|ja|本殿}} was reconstructed starting in 1873 and completed in 1877. During this time, it absorbed Hirogaki Shrine and Kijimi Shrine, which were nearby.

Rituals are conducted jointly with Ōmushi Shrine, including performances of kagura (sacred dances) and sword dances. These festivities are part of the annual "Kayadani Festival," held on the last weekend of April.

The shrine is situated on an eastern plateau of the Kayadani area along the midstream of the {{ill|Nodagawa River (Kyoto)|ja|野田川|12=draft}}, at the western base of the Ōeyama mountain range. Kayadani is known for its concentration of ancient Kofuns and archaeological sites, such as the nationally designated [[Hiruko-yama Kofun]] and the [[Sakuyama Kofun (Yosano Town)|Sakuyama Kofun]], making it a key area in the ancient [[Tango Province]].
== Deities == * {{ill|Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|12=draft}} (The shrine records from 1883 note an alternate tradition enshrining "Wakayama-kui no Mikoto." <ref>Included in Volume 1 of the "Kayachō History: Materials Edition."</ref>) * {{ill|Kagutsuchi no Mikoto|ja|火産霊命|1=Kagu-tsuchi|12=draft}} (from Hirogaki Shrine, merged in 1877) * {{ill|Ōyamatsumi no Mikoto|ja|大山祇命|1=Ōyamatsumi|12=draft}} (from Kijimi Shrine, merged simultaneously with Hirogaki Shrine)
The primary deity, {{ill|Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|12=draft}} (or Wakayama-kui no Mikoto), is regarded as the god responsible for the construction of Yosa District and the guardian deity of hot springs.<ref>Kayachō History. Regarding the current location of "Atsue," the [[Meiji period]] scholar {{ill|Yoshida Tōgo|ja|吉田東伍|12=draft}} suggested the name originated from the ancient presence of hot springs, although no springs remain as of the Meiji era.</ref>
== History == === Founding Tradition === The shrine was originally located in Ikegamaru, a site on the slopes of the Ōeyama mountain range, alongside the nearby {{ill|Ōmushi Shrine (Yosano Town)|ja|大虫神社 (与謝野町)|12=draft}}. In ancient times, {{ill|Ōkuninushi no Mikoto|ja|大国主命|12=draft}}, enshrined at Ōmushi Shrine, was said to reside in the area with {{ill|Numagawa-hime|ja|沼河姫|12=draft}}. According to legend, {{ill|Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|12=draft}} cured the poisoned Numagawa-hime, who had fallen ill due to the malice of a demon named Tsuchi-oni. This act gave rise to the worship of "Ōmushi" and "Komushi" as protector deities.<ref name="InvestigationReport">"Shikinai Shrine Investigation Report."</ref>
Additionally, the site is associated with the military expeditions of {{ill|Prince Maroko|ja|麻呂子親王|12=draft}}, a son of {{ill|Emperor Yōmei|ja|用明天皇|12=draft}}, who is said to have prayed here before subduing rebellious forces in Ōeyama.
=== Later History === Komushi Shrine is listed alongside Ōmushi Shrine as a {{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|12=draft}} in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式|12=draft}}. The shrine's relocation to its current site, along with Ōmushi Shrine, likely occurred in the early [[Muromachi period]], though the exact circumstances are unclear.<ref>Kayachō History. A remnant of the former site, known as "Mitarashi Pond," is said to exist.</ref>
In 1873, the shrine was classified as a [[Village Shrine]] under [[Toyooka Prefecture]]. The current {{ill|Main Hall|ja|本殿|12=draft}} was reconstructed starting in 1873 and completed in 1877. During this time, it absorbed Hirogaki Shrine and Kijimi Shrine, which were nearby.
== Rituals == The shrine's parishioners include six districts of Atsue: Omoriya, Onoue, Monde, Tanigaki, Osa, and Homura. Rituals are conducted jointly with Ōmushi Shrine, including performances of kagura (sacred dances) and sword dances. These festivities are part of the annual "Kayadani Festival," held on the last weekend of April.
== Structures == The {{ill|Main Hall|ja|本殿|12=draft}} was rebuilt in 1877 in the {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造|12=draft}} style. Other structures include the {{ill|Worship Hall|ja|拝殿|12=draft}}, a {{ill|Stage Pavilion|ja|舞殿|12=draft}}, and several auxiliary shrines, such as Wakamiya Shrine, Komori Shrine, and Nekomiya Shrine.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Kayachō Compilation Committee, ''Kayachō History,'' Kayachō Town Hall, 1974 * Kayachō Compilation Committee, ''Kayachō History: Materials Edition,'' Volumes 1 and 2, Yosano Town Hall, 2007-2008 * Shikinai Shrine Research Society, ''Shikinai Shrine Investigation Report,'' Volume 18: San'in Region 1, Kōgakkan University Press, 1984 * Tanikawa Ken'ichi, ed., ''Japanese Gods and Sacred Sites,'' Vol. 7: San'in Region, Hakusuisha, 2000
== External Links == * [http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/376001.html Komushi Shrine] (Kokugakuin University, "Shinto and Shrine Historical Resources")

It is a designated {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=draft}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大|12=draft}}) and ranked as a [[Village Shrine]] under the [[Modern typeing System]]. A remnant of the former site, known as "Mitarashi Pond," is said to exist.</ref>

'''Komushi Shrine''' ({{ill|Komushi Shrine|ja|小虫神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Komushi Shrine|qq=}}) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[Atsue]], [[Yosano Town]], [[Yosa District]], [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]]. It is a designated {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōjin Taisha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 名神大社}}) and Ranked as a [[Village Shrine]] under the [[Modern typeing System]]. The shrine is situated on an eastern plateau of the Kayadani area along the midstream of the {{ill|Nodagawa River (Kyoto)|ja|野田川|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nodagawa River (Kyoto)|qq=|1=Nodagawa River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11645972}}, at the western base of the Ōeyama mountain range. Kayadani is known for its concentration of ancient Kofuns and archaeological sites, such as the nationally designated [[Hiruko-yama Kofun]] and the [[Sakuyama Kofun (Yosano Town)|Sakuyama Kofun]], making it a key area in the ancient [[:en:Tango Province|Tango Province]].
== Deities == * {{ill|Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}} (The shrine records from 1883 note an alternate tradition enshrining "Wakayama-kui no Mikoto." <ref>Included in Volume 1 of the "Kayachō History: Materials Edition."</ref>) * {{ill|Kagutsuchi no Mikoto|ja|火産霊命|1=Kagu-tsuchi|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kagutsuchi no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Kagu-tsuchi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to カグツチ}} (from Hirogaki Shrine, merged in 1877) * {{ill|Ōyamatsumi no Mikoto|ja|大山祇命|1=Ōyamatsumi|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamatsumi no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamatsumi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to オオヤマツミ}} (from Kijimi Shrine, merged simultaneously with Hirogaki Shrine)
The primary deity, {{ill|Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}} (or Wakayama-kui no Mikoto), is regarded as the god responsible for the construction of Yosa District and the guardian deity of hot springs.<ref>Kayachō History. Regarding the current location of "Atsue," the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] scholar {{ill|Yoshida Tōgo|ja|吉田東伍|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshida Tōgo|qq=|1=Tōgo Yoshida|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11413910}} suggested the name originated from the ancient presence of hot springs, although no springs remain as of the Meiji era.</ref>
== History == === Founding Tradition === The shrine was originally located in Ikegamaru, a site on the slopes of the Ōeyama mountain range, alongside the nearby {{ill|Ōmushi Shrine (Yosano Town)|ja|大虫神社 (与謝野町)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmushi Shrine (Yosano Town)|qq=|1=Omushi Shrine (Yosano)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11438782|lt=Ōmushi Shrine (Yosano Town)}}. In ancient times, {{ill|Ōkuninushi no Mikoto|ja|大国主命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主|1=Ōkuninushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q276944}}, enshrined at Ōmushi Shrine, was said to reside in the area with {{ill|Numagawa-hime|ja|沼河姫|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Numagawa-hime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 沼河比売|1=Nunakawa hime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11142832}}. According to legend, {{ill|Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}} cured the poisoned Numagawa-hime, who had fallen ill due to the malice of a demon named Tsuchi-oni. This act gave rise to the worship of "Ōmushi" and "Komushi" as protector deities.<ref name="InvestigationReport">"Shikinai Shrine Investigation Report."</ref>
Additionally, the site is associated with the military expeditions of {{ill|Prince Maroko|ja|麻呂子親王|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Maroko|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 当麻皇子}}, a son of [[:en:Emperor Yōmei|Emperor Yōmei]], who is said to have prayed here before subduing rebellious forces in Ōeyama.
=== Later History === Komushi Shrine is listed alongside Ōmushi Shrine as a {{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōjin Taisha|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} in the [[:en:Engishiki|Engishiki]]. The shrine's relocation to its current site, along with Ōmushi Shrine, likely occurred in the early [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], though the exact circumstances are unclear.<ref>Kayachō History. A remnant of the former site, known as "Mitarashi Pond," is said to exist.</ref>
In 1873, the shrine was classified as a [[Village Shrine]] under [[Toyooka Prefecture]]. The current [[:en:Honden|Main Hall]] was reconstructed starting in 1873 and completed in 1877. During this time, it absorbed Hirogaki Shrine and Kijimi Shrine, which were nearby.
== Rituals == The shrine's parishioners include six districts of Atsue: Omoriya, Onoue, Monde, Tanigaki, Osa, and Homura. Rituals are conducted jointly with Ōmushi Shrine, including performances of kagura (sacred dances) and sword dances. These festivities are part of the annual "Kayadani Festival," held on the last weekend of April.
== Structures == The [[:en:Honden|Main Hall]] was rebuilt in 1877 in the [[:en:Shinmei-zukuri|Shinmei-zukuri]] style. Other structures include the [[:en:Haiden (Shinto)|Worship Hall]], a {{ill|Stage Pavilion|ja|舞殿|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Stage Pavilion|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and several auxiliary shrines, such as Wakamiya Shrine, Komori Shrine, and Nekomiya Shrine.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Komushi-jinja]][[ja:小虫神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11463552]] Wikidata name: Komushi-jinja [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:37, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11463552]] Wikidata name: Komushi-jinja [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:16, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Komushi Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = File:与謝野町温江 子虫神社.jpg |location = [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]], [[Yosa District]], [[Yosano Town]], Atsue, Omoriya 103 |coordinates = {{Coord|35.50075|N|135.108833|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|35|30|02.7|N|135|06|31.8|E|scale:25000 |deity = {{ill|Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}} |Rank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōjin Taisha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 名神大社}}), Former Village Shrine |founded = Unknown |main_hall = [[:en:Shinmei-zukuri|Shinmei-zukuri]] |alias = |festival = Last Sunday in April |ceremonies = Kayadani Festival (last Saturday and Sunday in April) |location = {{ill|Yosano|qid=Q1203259}} }}
'''Komushi Shrine''' ({{ill|Komushi Shrine|ja|小虫神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Komushi Shrine|qq=}}) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[Atsue]], [[Yosano Town]], [[Yosa District]], [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]]. It is a designated {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōjin Taisha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 名神大社}}) and Ranked as a [[Village Shrine]] under the [[Modern typeing System]]. The shrine is situated on an eastern plateau of the Kayadani area along the midstream of the {{ill|Nodagawa River (Kyoto)|ja|野田川|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nodagawa River (Kyoto)|qq=|1=Nodagawa River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11645972}}, at the western base of the Ōeyama mountain range. Kayadani is known for its concentration of ancient Kofuns and archaeological sites, such as the nationally designated [[Hiruko-yama Kofun]] and the [[Sakuyama Kofun (Yosano Town)|Sakuyama Kofun]], making it a key area in the ancient [[:en:Tango Province|Tango Province]].
== Deities == * {{ill|Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}} (The shrine records from 1883 note an alternate tradition enshrining "Wakayama-kui no Mikoto." <ref>Included in Volume 1 of the "Kayachō History: Materials Edition."</ref>) * {{ill|Kagutsuchi no Mikoto|ja|火産霊命|1=Kagu-tsuchi|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kagutsuchi no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Kagu-tsuchi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to カグツチ}} (from Hirogaki Shrine, merged in 1877) * {{ill|Ōyamatsumi no Mikoto|ja|大山祇命|1=Ōyamatsumi|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamatsumi no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamatsumi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to オオヤマツミ}} (from Kijimi Shrine, merged simultaneously with Hirogaki Shrine)
The primary deity, {{ill|Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}} (or Wakayama-kui no Mikoto), is regarded as the god responsible for the construction of Yosa District and the guardian deity of hot springs.<ref>Kayachō History. Regarding the current location of "Atsue," the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] scholar {{ill|Yoshida Tōgo|ja|吉田東伍|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshida Tōgo|qq=|1=Tōgo Yoshida|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11413910}} suggested the name originated from the ancient presence of hot springs, although no springs remain as of the Meiji era.</ref>
== History == === Founding Tradition === The shrine was originally located in Ikegamaru, a site on the slopes of the Ōeyama mountain range, alongside the nearby {{ill|Ōmushi Shrine (Yosano Town)|ja|大虫神社 (与謝野町)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmushi Shrine (Yosano Town)|qq=|1=Omushi Shrine (Yosano)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11438782|lt=Ōmushi Shrine (Yosano Town)}}. In ancient times, {{ill|Ōkuninushi no Mikoto|ja|大国主命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主|1=Ōkuninushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q276944}}, enshrined at Ōmushi Shrine, was said to reside in the area with {{ill|Numagawa-hime|ja|沼河姫|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Numagawa-hime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 沼河比売|1=Nunakawa hime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11142832}}. According to legend, {{ill|Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}} cured the poisoned Numagawa-hime, who had fallen ill due to the malice of a demon named Tsuchi-oni. This act gave rise to the worship of "Ōmushi" and "Komushi" as protector deities.<ref name="InvestigationReport">"Shikinai Shrine Investigation Report."</ref>
Additionally, the site is associated with the military expeditions of {{ill|Prince Maroko|ja|麻呂子親王|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Maroko|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 当麻皇子}}, a son of [[:en:Emperor Yōmei|Emperor Yōmei]], who is said to have prayed here before subduing rebellious forces in Ōeyama.
=== Later History === Komushi Shrine is listed alongside Ōmushi Shrine as a {{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōjin Taisha|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} in the [[:en:Engishiki|Engishiki]]. The shrine's relocation to its current site, along with Ōmushi Shrine, likely occurred in the early [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], though the exact circumstances are unclear.<ref>Kayachō History. A remnant of the former site, known as "Mitarashi Pond," is said to exist.</ref>
In 1873, the shrine was classified as a [[Village Shrine]] under [[Toyooka Prefecture]]. The current [[:en:Honden|Main Hall]] was reconstructed starting in 1873 and completed in 1877. During this time, it absorbed Hirogaki Shrine and Kijimi Shrine, which were nearby.
== Rituals == The shrine's parishioners include six districts of Atsue: Omoriya, Onoue, Monde, Tanigaki, Osa, and Homura. Rituals are conducted jointly with Ōmushi Shrine, including performances of kagura (sacred dances) and sword dances. These festivities are part of the annual "Kayadani Festival," held on the last weekend of April.
== Structures == The [[:en:Honden|Main Hall]] was rebuilt in 1877 in the [[:en:Shinmei-zukuri|Shinmei-zukuri]] style. Other structures include the [[:en:Haiden (Shinto)|Worship Hall]], a {{ill|Stage Pavilion|ja|舞殿|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Stage Pavilion|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and several auxiliary shrines, such as Wakamiya Shrine, Komori Shrine, and Nekomiya Shrine.

The shrine is situated on an eastern plateau of the Kayadani area along the midstream of the {{ill|Nodagawa River (Kyoto)|ja|野田川|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nodagawa River (Kyoto)|qq=|1=Nodagawa River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11645972}}, at the western base of the Ōeyama mountain range. Kayadani is known for its concentration of ancient Kofuns and archaeological sites, such as the nationally designated [[Hiruko-yama Kofun]] and the [[Sakuyama Kofun (Yosano Town)|Sakuyama Kofun]], making it a key area in the ancient [[:en:Tango Province|Tango Province]].
== Deities == * {{ill|Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}} (The shrine records from 1883 note an alternate tradition enshrining "Wakayama-kui no Mikoto." <ref>Included in Volume 1 of the "Kayachō History: Materials Edition."</ref>) * {{ill|Kagutsuchi no Mikoto|ja|火産霊命|1=Kagu-tsuchi|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kagutsuchi no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Kagu-tsuchi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to カグツチ}} (from Hirogaki Shrine, merged in 1877) * {{ill|Ōyamatsumi no Mikoto|ja|大山祇命|1=Ōyamatsumi|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamatsumi no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamatsumi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to オオヤマツミ}} (from Kijimi Shrine, merged simultaneously with Hirogaki Shrine)
The primary deity, {{ill|Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}} (or Wakayama-kui no Mikoto), is regarded as the god responsible for the construction of Yosa District and the guardian deity of hot springs.<ref>Kayachō History. Regarding the current location of "Atsue," the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] scholar {{ill|Yoshida Tōgo|ja|吉田東伍|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshida Tōgo|qq=|1=Tōgo Yoshida|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11413910}} suggested the name originated from the ancient presence of hot springs, although no springs remain as of the Meiji era.</ref>
== History == === Founding Tradition === The shrine was originally located in Ikegamaru, a site on the slopes of the Ōeyama mountain range, alongside the nearby {{ill|Ōmushi Shrine (Yosano Town)|ja|大虫神社 (与謝野町)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmushi Shrine (Yosano Town)|qq=|1=Omushi Shrine (Yosano)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11438782|lt=Ōmushi Shrine (Yosano Town)}}. In ancient times, {{ill|Ōkuninushi no Mikoto|ja|大国主命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主|1=Ōkuninushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q276944}}, enshrined at Ōmushi Shrine, was said to reside in the area with {{ill|Numagawa-hime|ja|沼河姫|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Numagawa-hime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 沼河比売|1=Nunakawa hime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11142832}}. According to legend, {{ill|Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}} cured the poisoned Numagawa-hime, who had fallen ill due to the malice of a demon named Tsuchi-oni. This act gave rise to the worship of "Ōmushi" and "Komushi" as protector deities.<ref name="InvestigationReport">"Shikinai Shrine Investigation Report."</ref>
Additionally, the site is associated with the military expeditions of {{ill|Prince Maroko|ja|麻呂子親王|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Maroko|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 当麻皇子}}, a son of [[:en:Emperor Yōmei|Emperor Yōmei]], who is said to have prayed here before subduing rebellious forces in Ōeyama.
=== Later History === Komushi Shrine is listed alongside Ōmushi Shrine as a {{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōjin Taisha|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} in the [[:en:Engishiki|Engishiki]]. The shrine's relocation to its current site, along with Ōmushi Shrine, likely occurred in the early [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], though the exact circumstances are unclear.<ref>Kayachō History. A remnant of the former site, known as "Mitarashi Pond," is said to exist.</ref>
In 1873, the shrine was classified as a [[Village Shrine]] under [[Toyooka Prefecture]]. The current [[:en:Honden|Main Hall]] was reconstructed starting in 1873 and completed in 1877. During this time, it absorbed Hirogaki Shrine and Kijimi Shrine, which were nearby.
== Rituals == The shrine's parishioners include six districts of Atsue: Omoriya, Onoue, Monde, Tanigaki, Osa, and Homura. Rituals are conducted jointly with Ōmushi Shrine, including performances of kagura (sacred dances) and sword dances. These festivities are part of the annual "Kayadani Festival," held on the last weekend of April.
== Structures == The [[:en:Honden|Main Hall]] was rebuilt in 1877 in the [[:en:Shinmei-zukuri|Shinmei-zukuri]] style. Other structures include the [[:en:Haiden (Shinto)|Worship Hall]], a {{ill|Stage Pavilion|ja|舞殿|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Stage Pavilion|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and several auxiliary shrines, such as Wakamiya Shrine, Komori Shrine, and Nekomiya Shrine.

=== Later History === Komushi Shrine is listed alongside Ōmushi Shrine as a {{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōjin Taisha|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} in the [[:en:Engishiki|Engishiki]]. The shrine's relocation to its current site, along with Ōmushi Shrine, likely occurred in the early [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], though the exact circumstances are unclear.<ref>Kayachō History. A remnant of the former site, known as "Mitarashi Pond," is said to exist.</ref>
In 1873, the shrine was classified as a [[Village Shrine]] under [[Toyooka Prefecture]]. The current [[:en:Honden|Main Hall]] was reconstructed starting in 1873 and completed in 1877. During this time, it absorbed Hirogaki Shrine and Kijimi Shrine, which were nearby.
== Rituals == The shrine's parishioners include six districts of Atsue: Omoriya, Onoue, Monde, Tanigaki, Osa, and Homura. Rituals are conducted jointly with Ōmushi Shrine, including performances of kagura (sacred dances) and sword dances. These festivities are part of the annual "Kayadani Festival," held on the last weekend of April.
== Structures == The [[:en:Honden|Main Hall]] was rebuilt in 1877 in the [[:en:Shinmei-zukuri|Shinmei-zukuri]] style. Other structures include the [[:en:Haiden (Shinto)|Worship Hall]], a {{ill|Stage Pavilion|ja|舞殿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Stage Pavilion|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and several auxiliary shrines, such as Wakamiya Shrine, Komori Shrine, and Nekomiya Shrine.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Kayachō Compilation Committee, ''Kayachō History,'' Kayachō Town Hall, 1974 * Kayachō Compilation Committee, ''Kayachō History: Materials Edition,'' Volumes 1 and 2, Yosano Town Hall, 2007-2008 * Shikinai Shrine Research Society, ''Shikinai Shrine Investigation Report,'' Volume 18: San'in Region 1, Kōgakkan University Press, 1984 * Tanikawa Ken'ichi, ed., ''Japanese Gods and Sacred Sites,'' Vol. 7: San'in Region, Hakusuisha, 2000
== External Links == * [http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/376001.html Komushi Shrine] (Kokugakuin University, "Shinto and Shrine Historical Resources")

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Komushi-jinja]][[ja:小虫神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11463552]] Wikidata name: Komushi-jinja [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:37, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11463552]] Wikidata name: Komushi-jinja [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:16, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Komushi Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = File:与謝野町温江 子虫神社.jpg |location = [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]], [[Yosa District]], [[Yosano Town]], Atsue, Omoriya 103 |coordinates = {{Coord|35.50075|N|135.108833|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|35|30|02.7|N|135|06|31.8|E|scale:25000 |deity = {{ill|Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}} |Rank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōjin Taisha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 名神大社}}), Former Village Shrine |founded = Unknown |main_hall = [[:en:Shinmei-zukuri|Shinmei-zukuri]] |alias = |festival = Last Sunday in April |ceremonies = Kayadani Festival (last Saturday and Sunday in April) |location = {{ill|Yosano|WD=Q1203259}} }}
'''Komushi Shrine''' ({{ill|Komushi Shrine|ja|小虫神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Komushi Shrine|qq=}}) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[Atsue]], [[Yosano Town]], [[Yosa District]], [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]]. It is a designated {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōjin Taisha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 名神大社}}) and Ranked as a [[Village Shrine]] under the [[Modern typeing System]]. The shrine is situated on an eastern plateau of the Kayadani area along the midstream of the {{ill|Nodagawa River (Kyoto)|ja|野田川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nodagawa River (Kyoto)|1=Nodagawa River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11645972}}, at the western base of the Ōeyama mountain range. Kayadani is known for its concentration of ancient Kofuns and archaeological sites, such as the nationally designated [[Hiruko-yama Kofun]] and the [[Sakuyama Kofun (Yosano Town)|Sakuyama Kofun]], making it a key area in the ancient [[:en:Tango Province|Tango Province]].
== Deities == * {{ill|Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}} (The shrine records from 1883 note an alternate tradition enshrining "Wakayama-kui no Mikoto." <ref>Included in Volume 1 of the "Kayachō History: Materials Edition."</ref>) * {{ill|Kagutsuchi no Mikoto|ja|火産霊命|1=Kagu-tsuchi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kagutsuchi no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Kagu-tsuchi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to カグツチ}} (from Hirogaki Shrine, merged in 1877) * {{ill|Ōyamatsumi no Mikoto|ja|大山祇命|1=Ōyamatsumi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamatsumi no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamatsumi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to オオヤマツミ}} (from Kijimi Shrine, merged simultaneously with Hirogaki Shrine)
The primary deity, {{ill|Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}} (or Wakayama-kui no Mikoto), is regarded as the god responsible for the construction of Yosa District and the guardian deity of hot springs.<ref>Kayachō History. Regarding the current location of "Atsue," the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] scholar {{ill|Yoshida Tōgo|ja|吉田東伍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshida Tōgo|1=Tōgo Yoshida|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11413910}} suggested the name originated from the ancient presence of hot springs, although no springs remain as of the Meiji era.</ref>
== History == === Founding Tradition === The shrine was originally located in Ikegamaru, a site on the slopes of the Ōeyama mountain range, alongside the nearby {{ill|Ōmushi Shrine (Yosano Town)|ja|大虫神社 (与謝野町)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmushi Shrine (Yosano Town)|1=Omushi Shrine (Yosano)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11438782|lt=Ōmushi Shrine (Yosano Town)}}. In ancient times, {{ill|Ōkuninushi no Mikoto|ja|大国主命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主|1=Ōkuninushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q276944}}, enshrined at Ōmushi Shrine, was said to reside in the area with {{ill|Numagawa-hime|ja|沼河姫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Numagawa-hime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 沼河比売|1=Nunakawa hime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11142832}}. According to legend, {{ill|Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}} cured the poisoned Numagawa-hime, who had fallen ill due to the malice of a demon named Tsuchi-oni. This act gave rise to the worship of "Ōmushi" and "Komushi" as protector deities.<ref name="InvestigationReport">"Shikinai Shrine Investigation Report."</ref>
Additionally, the site is associated with the military expeditions of {{ill|Prince Maroko|ja|麻呂子親王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Maroko|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 当麻皇子}}, a son of [[:en:Emperor Yōmei|Emperor Yōmei]], who is said to have prayed here before subduing rebellious forces in Ōeyama.
=== Later History === Komushi Shrine is listed alongside Ōmushi Shrine as a {{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōjin Taisha|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} in the [[:en:Engishiki|Engishiki]]. The shrine's relocation to its current site, along with Ōmushi Shrine, likely occurred in the early [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], though the exact circumstances are unclear.<ref>Kayachō History. A remnant of the former site, known as "Mitarashi Pond," is said to exist.</ref>
In 1873, the shrine was classified as a [[Village Shrine]] under [[Toyooka Prefecture]]. The current [[:en:Honden|Main Hall]] was reconstructed starting in 1873 and completed in 1877. During this time, it absorbed Hirogaki Shrine and Kijimi Shrine, which were nearby.
== Rituals == The shrine's parishioners include six districts of Atsue: Omoriya, Onoue, Monde, Tanigaki, Osa, and Homura. Rituals are conducted jointly with Ōmushi Shrine, including performances of kagura (sacred dances) and sword dances. These festivities are part of the annual "Kayadani Festival," held on the last weekend of April.
== Structures == The [[:en:Honden|Main Hall]] was rebuilt in 1877 in the [[:en:Shinmei-zukuri|Shinmei-zukuri]] style. Other structures include the [[:en:Haiden (Shinto)|Worship Hall]], a {{ill|Stage Pavilion|ja|舞殿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Stage Pavilion|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and several auxiliary shrines, such as Wakamiya Shrine, Komori Shrine, and Nekomiya Shrine.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Kayachō Compilation Committee, ''Kayachō History,'' Kayachō Town Hall, 1974 * Kayachō Compilation Committee, ''Kayachō History: Materials Edition,'' Volumes 1 and 2, Yosano Town Hall, 2007-2008 * Shikinai Shrine Research Society, ''Shikinai Shrine Investigation Report,'' Volume 18: San'in Region 1, Kōgakkan University Press, 1984 * Tanikawa Ken'ichi, ed., ''Japanese Gods and Sacred Sites,'' Vol. 7: San'in Region, Hakusuisha, 2000
== External Links == * [http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/376001.html Komushi Shrine] (Kokugakuin University, "Shinto and Shrine Historical Resources")

The shrine is situated on an eastern plateau of the Kayadani area along the midstream of the {{ill|Nodagawa River (Kyoto)|ja|野田川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nodagawa River (Kyoto)|1=Nodagawa River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11645972}}, at the western base of the Ōeyama mountain range. A remnant of the former site, known as "Mitarashi Pond," is said to exist.</ref>

'''Komushi Shrine''' ({{ill|Komushi Shrine|ja|小虫神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Komushi Shrine|qq=}}) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[Atsue]], [[Yosano Town]], [[Yosa District]], [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]]. It is a designated {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōjin Taisha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 名神大社}}) and Ranked as a [[Village Shrine]] under the [[Modern typeing System]]. The shrine is situated on an eastern plateau of the Kayadani area along the midstream of the {{ill|Nodagawa River (Kyoto)|ja|野田川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nodagawa River (Kyoto)|1=Nodagawa River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11645972}}, at the western base of the Ōeyama mountain range. Kayadani is known for its concentration of ancient Kofuns and archaeological sites, such as the nationally designated [[Hiruko-yama Kofun]] and the [[Sakuyama Kofun (Yosano Town)|Sakuyama Kofun]], making it a key area in the ancient [[:en:Tango Province|Tango Province]].
== Deities == * {{ill|Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}} (The shrine records from 1883 note an alternate tradition enshrining "Wakayama-kui no Mikoto." <ref>Included in Volume 1 of the "Kayachō History: Materials Edition."</ref>) * {{ill|Kagutsuchi no Mikoto|ja|火産霊命|1=Kagu-tsuchi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kagutsuchi no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Kagu-tsuchi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to カグツチ}} (from Hirogaki Shrine, merged in 1877) * {{ill|Ōyamatsumi no Mikoto|ja|大山祇命|1=Ōyamatsumi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamatsumi no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamatsumi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to オオヤマツミ}} (from Kijimi Shrine, merged simultaneously with Hirogaki Shrine)
The primary deity, {{ill|Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}} (or Wakayama-kui no Mikoto), is regarded as the god responsible for the construction of Yosa District and the guardian deity of hot springs.<ref>Kayachō History. Regarding the current location of "Atsue," the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] scholar {{ill|Yoshida Tōgo|ja|吉田東伍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshida Tōgo|1=Tōgo Yoshida|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11413910}} suggested the name originated from the ancient presence of hot springs, although no springs remain as of the Meiji era.</ref>
== History == === Founding Tradition === The shrine was originally located in Ikegamaru, a site on the slopes of the Ōeyama mountain range, alongside the nearby {{ill|Ōmushi Shrine (Yosano Town)|ja|大虫神社 (与謝野町)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmushi Shrine (Yosano Town)|1=Omushi Shrine (Yosano)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11438782|lt=Ōmushi Shrine (Yosano Town)}}. In ancient times, {{ill|Ōkuninushi no Mikoto|ja|大国主命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主|1=Ōkuninushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q276944}}, enshrined at Ōmushi Shrine, was said to reside in the area with {{ill|Numagawa-hime|ja|沼河姫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Numagawa-hime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 沼河比売|1=Nunakawa hime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11142832}}. According to legend, {{ill|Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}} cured the poisoned Numagawa-hime, who had fallen ill due to the malice of a demon named Tsuchi-oni. This act gave rise to the worship of "Ōmushi" and "Komushi" as protector deities.<ref name="InvestigationReport">"Shikinai Shrine Investigation Report."</ref>
Additionally, the site is associated with the military expeditions of {{ill|Prince Maroko|ja|麻呂子親王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Maroko|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 当麻皇子}}, a son of [[:en:Emperor Yōmei|Emperor Yōmei]], who is said to have prayed here before subduing rebellious forces in Ōeyama.
=== Later History === Komushi Shrine is listed alongside Ōmushi Shrine as a {{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōjin Taisha|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} in the [[:en:Engishiki|Engishiki]]. The shrine's relocation to its current site, along with Ōmushi Shrine, likely occurred in the early [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], though the exact circumstances are unclear.<ref>Kayachō History. A remnant of the former site, known as "Mitarashi Pond," is said to exist.</ref>
In 1873, the shrine was classified as a [[Village Shrine]] under [[Toyooka Prefecture]]. The current [[:en:Honden|Main Hall]] was reconstructed starting in 1873 and completed in 1877. During this time, it absorbed Hirogaki Shrine and Kijimi Shrine, which were nearby.
== Rituals == The shrine's parishioners include six districts of Atsue: Omoriya, Onoue, Monde, Tanigaki, Osa, and Homura. Rituals are conducted jointly with Ōmushi Shrine, including performances of kagura (sacred dances) and sword dances. These festivities are part of the annual "Kayadani Festival," held on the last weekend of April.
== Structures == The [[:en:Honden|Main Hall]] was rebuilt in 1877 in the [[:en:Shinmei-zukuri|Shinmei-zukuri]] style. Other structures include the [[:en:Haiden (Shinto)|Worship Hall]], a {{ill|Stage Pavilion|ja|舞殿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Stage Pavilion|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and several auxiliary shrines, such as Wakamiya Shrine, Komori Shrine, and Nekomiya Shrine.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Kayachō Compilation Committee, ''Kayachō History,'' Kayachō Town Hall, 1974 * Kayachō Compilation Committee, ''Kayachō History: Materials Edition,'' Volumes 1 and 2, Yosano Town Hall, 2007-2008 * Shikinai Shrine Research Society, ''Shikinai Shrine Investigation Report,'' Volume 18: San'in Region 1, Kōgakkan University Press, 1984 * Tanikawa Ken'ichi, ed., ''Japanese Gods and Sacred Sites,'' Vol. 7: San'in Region, Hakusuisha, 2000

== Deities == * {{ill|Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}} (The shrine records from 1883 note an alternate tradition enshrining "Wakayama-kui no Mikoto." <ref>Included in Volume 1 of the "Kayachō History: Materials Edition."</ref>) * {{ill|Kagutsuchi no Mikoto|ja|火産霊命|1=Kagu-tsuchi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kagutsuchi no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Kagu-tsuchi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to カグツチ}} (from Hirogaki Shrine, merged in 1877) * {{ill|Ōyamatsumi no Mikoto|ja|大山祇命|1=Ōyamatsumi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamatsumi no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamatsumi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to オオヤマツミ}} (from Kijimi Shrine, merged simultaneously with Hirogaki Shrine)
The primary deity, {{ill|Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}} (or Wakayama-kui no Mikoto), is regarded as the god responsible for the construction of Yosa District and the guardian deity of hot springs.<ref>Kayachō History. Regarding the current location of "Atsue," the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] scholar {{ill|Yoshida Tōgo|ja|吉田東伍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshida Tōgo|1=Tōgo Yoshida|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11413910}} suggested the name originated from the ancient presence of hot springs, although no springs remain as of the Meiji era.</ref>
== History == === Founding Tradition === The shrine was originally located in Ikegamaru, a site on the slopes of the Ōeyama mountain range, alongside the nearby {{ill|Ōmushi Shrine (Yosano Town)|ja|大虫神社 (与謝野町)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmushi Shrine (Yosano Town)|1=Omushi Shrine (Yosano)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11438782|lt=Ōmushi Shrine (Yosano Town)}}. In ancient times, {{ill|Ōkuninushi no Mikoto|ja|大国主命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主|1=Ōkuninushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q276944}}, enshrined at Ōmushi Shrine, was said to reside in the area with {{ill|Numagawa-hime|ja|沼河姫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Numagawa-hime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 沼河比売|1=Nunakawa hime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11142832}}. According to legend, {{ill|Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}} cured the poisoned Numagawa-hime, who had fallen ill due to the malice of a demon named Tsuchi-oni. This act gave rise to the worship of "Ōmushi" and "Komushi" as protector deities.<ref name="InvestigationReport">"Shikinai Shrine Investigation Report."</ref>
Additionally, the site is associated with the military expeditions of {{ill|Prince Maroko|ja|麻呂子親王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Maroko|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 当麻皇子}}, a son of [[:en:Emperor Yōmei|Emperor Yōmei]], who is said to have prayed here before subduing rebellious forces in Ōeyama.
=== Later History === Komushi Shrine is listed alongside Ōmushi Shrine as a {{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōjin Taisha|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} in the [[:en:Engishiki|Engishiki]]. The shrine's relocation to its current site, along with Ōmushi Shrine, likely occurred in the early [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], though the exact circumstances are unclear.<ref>Kayachō History. A remnant of the former site, known as "Mitarashi Pond," is said to exist.</ref>
In 1873, the shrine was classified as a [[Village Shrine]] under [[Toyooka Prefecture]]. The current [[:en:Honden|Main Hall]] was reconstructed starting in 1873 and completed in 1877. During this time, it absorbed Hirogaki Shrine and Kijimi Shrine, which were nearby.
== Rituals == The shrine's parishioners include six districts of Atsue: Omoriya, Onoue, Monde, Tanigaki, Osa, and Homura. Rituals are conducted jointly with Ōmushi Shrine, including performances of kagura (sacred dances) and sword dances. These festivities are part of the annual "Kayadani Festival," held on the last weekend of April.
== Structures == The [[:en:Honden|Main Hall]] was rebuilt in 1877 in the [[:en:Shinmei-zukuri|Shinmei-zukuri]] style. Other structures include the [[:en:Haiden (Shinto)|Worship Hall]], a {{ill|Stage Pavilion|ja|舞殿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Stage Pavilion|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and several auxiliary shrines, such as Wakamiya Shrine, Komori Shrine, and Nekomiya Shrine.

'''Komushi Shrine''' ({{ill|Komushi Shrine|ja|小虫神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Komushi Shrine|qq=}}) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[Atsue]], [[Yosano Town]], [[Yosa District]], [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]]. It is a designated {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōjin Taisha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 名神大社}}) and Ranked as a [[Village Shrine]] under the [[Modern typeing System]]. The shrine is situated on an eastern plateau of the Kayadani area along the midstream of the {{ill|Nodagawa River (Kyoto)|ja|野田川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nodagawa River (Kyoto)|1=Nodagawa River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11645972}}, at the western base of the Ōeyama mountain range. Kayadani is known for its concentration of ancient Kofuns and archaeological sites, such as the nationally designated [[Hiruko-yama Kofun]] and the [[Sakuyama Kofun (Yosano Town)|Sakuyama Kofun]], making it a key area in the ancient [[:en:Tango Province|Tango Province]].

Rituals are conducted jointly with Ōmushi Shrine, including performances of kagura (sacred dances) and sword dances. These festivities are part of the annual "Kayadani Festival," held on the last weekend of April.
== Structures == The [[:en:Honden|Main Hall]] was rebuilt in 1877 in the [[:en:Shinmei-zukuri|Shinmei-zukuri]] style. Other structures include the [[:en:Haiden (Shinto)|Worship Hall]], a {{ill|Stage Pavilion|ja|舞殿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Stage Pavilion|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and several auxiliary shrines, such as Wakamiya Shrine, Komori Shrine, and Nekomiya Shrine.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Komushi-jinja]][[ja:小虫神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11463552]] Wikidata name: Komushi-jinja [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:37, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11463552]] Wikidata name: Komushi-jinja [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:16, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Komushi Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = File:与謝野町温江 子虫神社.jpg |location = [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]], [[Yosa District]], [[Yosano Town]], Atsue, Omoriya 103 |coordinates = {{Coord|35.50075|N|135.108833|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|35|30|02.7|N|135|06|31.8|E|scale:25000 |deity = {{ill|Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}} |Rank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōjin Taisha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 名神大社}}), Former Village Shrine |founded = Unknown |main_hall = [[:en:Shinmei-zukuri|Shinmei-zukuri]] |alias = |festival = Last Sunday in April |ceremonies = Kayadani Festival (last Saturday and Sunday in April) |location = {{ill|Yosano|WD=Q1203259}} }}
'''Komushi Shrine''' ({{ill|Komushi Shrine|ja|小虫神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Komushi Shrine|qq=}}) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[Atsue]], [[Yosano Town]], [[Yosa District]], [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]]. It is a designated {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōjin Taisha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 名神大社}}) and Ranked as a [[Village Shrine]] under the [[Modern typeing System]]. The shrine is situated on an eastern plateau of the Kayadani area along the midstream of the {{ill|Nodagawa River (Kyoto)|ja|野田川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nodagawa River (Kyoto)|1=Nodagawa River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11645972}}, at the western base of the Ōeyama mountain range. Kayadani is known for its concentration of ancient Kofuns and archaeological sites, such as the nationally designated [[Hiruko-yama Kofun]] and the [[Sakuyama Kofun (Yosano Town)|Sakuyama Kofun]], making it a key area in the ancient [[:en:Tango Province|Tango Province]].
== Deities == * {{ill|Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}} (The shrine records from 1883 note an alternate tradition enshrining "Wakayama-kui no Mikoto." <ref>Included in Volume 1 of the "Kayachō History: Materials Edition."</ref>) * {{ill|Kagutsuchi no Mikoto|ja|火産霊命|1=Kagu-tsuchi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kagutsuchi no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Kagu-tsuchi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to カグツチ}} (from Hirogaki Shrine, merged in 1877) * {{ill|Ōyamatsumi no Mikoto|ja|大山祇命|1=Ōyamatsumi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamatsumi no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamatsumi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to オオヤマツミ}} (from Kijimi Shrine, merged simultaneously with Hirogaki Shrine)
The primary deity, {{ill|Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}} (or Wakayama-kui no Mikoto), is regarded as the god responsible for the construction of Yosa District and the guardian deity of hot springs.<ref>Kayachō History. Regarding the current location of "Atsue," the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] scholar {{ill|Yoshida Tōgo|ja|吉田東伍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshida Tōgo|1=Tōgo Yoshida|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11413910}} suggested the name originated from the ancient presence of hot springs, although no springs remain as of the Meiji era.</ref>
== History == === Founding Tradition === The shrine was originally located in Ikegamaru, a site on the slopes of the Ōeyama mountain range, alongside the nearby {{ill|Ōmushi Shrine (Yosano Town)|ja|大虫神社 (与謝野町)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmushi Shrine (Yosano Town)|1=Omushi Shrine (Yosano)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11438782|lt=Ōmushi Shrine (Yosano Town)}}. In ancient times, {{ill|Ōkuninushi no Mikoto|ja|大国主命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主|1=Ōkuninushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q276944}}, enshrined at Ōmushi Shrine, was said to reside in the area with {{ill|Numagawa-hime|ja|沼河姫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Numagawa-hime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 沼河比売|1=Nunakawa hime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11142832}}. According to legend, {{ill|Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}} cured the poisoned Numagawa-hime, who had fallen ill due to the malice of a demon named Tsuchi-oni. This act gave rise to the worship of "Ōmushi" and "Komushi" as protector deities.<ref name="InvestigationReport">"Shikinai Shrine Investigation Report."</ref>
Additionally, the site is associated with the military expeditions of {{ill|Prince Maroko|ja|麻呂子親王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Maroko|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 当麻皇子}}, a son of [[:en:Emperor Yōmei|Emperor Yōmei]], who is said to have prayed here before subduing rebellious forces in Ōeyama.
=== Later History === Komushi Shrine is listed alongside Ōmushi Shrine as a {{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōjin Taisha|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} in the [[:en:Engishiki|Engishiki]]. The shrine's relocation to its current site, along with Ōmushi Shrine, likely occurred in the early [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], though the exact circumstances are unclear.<ref>Kayachō History. A remnant of the former site, known as "Mitarashi Pond," is said to exist.</ref>
In 1873, the shrine was classified as a [[Village Shrine]] under [[Toyooka Prefecture]]. The current [[:en:Honden|Main Hall]] was reconstructed starting in 1873 and completed in 1877. During this time, it absorbed Hirogaki Shrine and Kijimi Shrine, which were nearby.
== Rituals == The shrine's parishioners include six districts of Atsue: Omoriya, Onoue, Monde, Tanigaki, Osa, and Homura. Rituals are conducted jointly with Ōmushi Shrine, including performances of kagura (sacred dances) and sword dances. These festivities are part of the annual "Kayadani Festival," held on the last weekend of April.
== Structures == The [[:en:Honden|Main Hall]] was rebuilt in 1877 in the [[:en:Shinmei-zukuri|Shinmei-zukuri]] style. Other structures include the [[:en:Haiden (Shinto)|Worship Hall]], a {{ill|Stage Pavilion|ja|舞殿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Stage Pavilion|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and several auxiliary shrines, such as Wakamiya Shrine, Komori Shrine, and Nekomiya Shrine.

'''Komushi Shrine''' ({{ill|Komushi Shrine|ja|小虫神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Komushi Shrine|qq=}}) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[Atsue]], [[Yosano Town]], [[Yosa District]], [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]]. It is a designated {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōjin Taisha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 名神大社}}) and Ranked as a [[Village Shrine]] under the [[Modern typeing System]]. The shrine is situated on an eastern plateau of the Kayadani area along the midstream of the {{ill|Nodagawa River (Kyoto)|ja|野田川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nodagawa River (Kyoto)|1=Nodagawa River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11645972}}, at the western base of the Ōeyama mountain range. Kayadani is known for its concentration of ancient Kofuns and archaeological sites, such as the nationally designated [[Hiruko-yama Kofun]] and the [[Sakuyama Kofun (Yosano Town)|Sakuyama Kofun]], making it a key area in the ancient [[:en:Tango Province|Tango Province]].
== Deities == * {{ill|Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}} (The shrine records from 1883 note an alternate tradition enshrining "Wakayama-kui no Mikoto." <ref>Included in Volume 1 of the "Kayachō History: Materials Edition."</ref>) * {{ill|Kagutsuchi no Mikoto|ja|火産霊命|1=Kagu-tsuchi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kagutsuchi no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Kagu-tsuchi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to カグツチ}} (from Hirogaki Shrine, merged in 1877) * {{ill|Ōyamatsumi no Mikoto|ja|大山祇命|1=Ōyamatsumi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamatsumi no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamatsumi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to オオヤマツミ}} (from Kijimi Shrine, merged simultaneously with Hirogaki Shrine)
The primary deity, {{ill|Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}} (or Wakayama-kui no Mikoto), is regarded as the god responsible for the construction of Yosa District and the guardian deity of hot springs.<ref>Kayachō History. Regarding the current location of "Atsue," the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] scholar {{ill|Yoshida Tōgo|ja|吉田東伍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshida Tōgo|1=Tōgo Yoshida|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11413910}} suggested the name originated from the ancient presence of hot springs, although no springs remain as of the Meiji era.</ref>
== History == === Founding Tradition === The shrine was originally located in Ikegamaru, a site on the slopes of the Ōeyama mountain range, alongside the nearby {{ill|Ōmushi Shrine (Yosano Town)|ja|大虫神社 (与謝野町)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmushi Shrine (Yosano Town)|1=Omushi Shrine (Yosano)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11438782|lt=Ōmushi Shrine (Yosano Town)}}. In ancient times, {{ill|Ōkuninushi no Mikoto|ja|大国主命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主|1=Ōkuninushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q276944}}, enshrined at Ōmushi Shrine, was said to reside in the area with {{ill|Numagawa-hime|ja|沼河姫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Numagawa-hime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 沼河比売|1=Nunakawa hime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11142832}}. According to legend, {{ill|Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}} cured the poisoned Numagawa-hime, who had fallen ill due to the malice of a demon named Tsuchi-oni. This act gave rise to the worship of "Ōmushi" and "Komushi" as protector deities.<ref name="InvestigationReport">"Shikinai Shrine Investigation Report."</ref>
Additionally, the site is associated with the military expeditions of {{ill|Prince Maroko|ja|麻呂子親王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Maroko|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 当麻皇子}}, a son of [[:en:Emperor Yōmei|Emperor Yōmei]], who is said to have prayed here before subduing rebellious forces in Ōeyama.
=== Later History === Komushi Shrine is listed alongside Ōmushi Shrine as a {{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōjin Taisha|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} in the [[:en:Engishiki|Engishiki]]. The shrine's relocation to its current site, along with Ōmushi Shrine, likely occurred in the early [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], though the exact circumstances are unclear.<ref>Kayachō History. A remnant of the former site, known as "Mitarashi Pond," is said to exist.</ref>
In 1873, the shrine was classified as a [[Village Shrine]] under [[Toyooka Prefecture]]. The current [[:en:Honden|Main Hall]] was reconstructed starting in 1873 and completed in 1877. During this time, it absorbed Hirogaki Shrine and Kijimi Shrine, which were nearby.
== Rituals == The shrine's parishioners include six districts of Atsue: Omoriya, Onoue, Monde, Tanigaki, Osa, and Homura. Rituals are conducted jointly with Ōmushi Shrine, including performances of kagura (sacred dances) and sword dances. These festivities are part of the annual "Kayadani Festival," held on the last weekend of April.
== Structures == The [[:en:Honden|Main Hall]] was rebuilt in 1877 in the [[:en:Shinmei-zukuri|Shinmei-zukuri]] style. Other structures include the [[:en:Haiden (Shinto)|Worship Hall]], a {{ill|Stage Pavilion|ja|舞殿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Stage Pavilion|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and several auxiliary shrines, such as Wakamiya Shrine, Komori Shrine, and Nekomiya Shrine.

The shrine is situated on an eastern plateau of the Kayadani area along the midstream of the {{ill|Nodagawa River (Kyoto)|ja|野田川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nodagawa River (Kyoto)|1=Nodagawa River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11645972}}, at the western base of the Ōeyama mountain range. Kayadani is known for its concentration of ancient Kofuns and archaeological sites, such as the nationally designated [[Hiruko-yama Kofun]] and the [[Sakuyama Kofun (Yosano Town)|Sakuyama Kofun]], making it a key area in the ancient [[:en:Tango Province|Tango Province]].
== Deities == * {{ill|Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}} (The shrine records from 1883 note an alternate tradition enshrining "Wakayama-kui no Mikoto." <ref>Included in Volume 1 of the "Kayachō History: Materials Edition."</ref>) * {{ill|Kagutsuchi no Mikoto|ja|火産霊命|1=Kagu-tsuchi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kagutsuchi no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Kagu-tsuchi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to カグツチ}} (from Hirogaki Shrine, merged in 1877) * {{ill|Ōyamatsumi no Mikoto|ja|大山祇命|1=Ōyamatsumi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamatsumi no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamatsumi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to オオヤマツミ}} (from Kijimi Shrine, merged simultaneously with Hirogaki Shrine)
The primary deity, {{ill|Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}} (or Wakayama-kui no Mikoto), is regarded as the god responsible for the construction of Yosa District and the guardian deity of hot springs.<ref>Kayachō History. Regarding the current location of "Atsue," the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] scholar {{ill|Yoshida Tōgo|ja|吉田東伍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshida Tōgo|1=Tōgo Yoshida|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11413910}} suggested the name originated from the ancient presence of hot springs, although no springs remain as of the Meiji era.</ref>
== History == === Founding Tradition === The shrine was originally located in Ikegamaru, a site on the slopes of the Ōeyama mountain range, alongside the nearby {{ill|Ōmushi Shrine (Yosano Town)|ja|大虫神社 (与謝野町)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmushi Shrine (Yosano Town)|1=Omushi Shrine (Yosano)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11438782|lt=Ōmushi Shrine (Yosano Town)}}. In ancient times, {{ill|Ōkuninushi no Mikoto|ja|大国主命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主|1=Ōkuninushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q276944}}, enshrined at Ōmushi Shrine, was said to reside in the area with {{ill|Numagawa-hime|ja|沼河姫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Numagawa-hime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 沼河比売|1=Nunakawa hime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11142832}}. According to legend, {{ill|Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}} cured the poisoned Numagawa-hime, who had fallen ill due to the malice of a demon named Tsuchi-oni. This act gave rise to the worship of "Ōmushi" and "Komushi" as protector deities.<ref name="InvestigationReport">"Shikinai Shrine Investigation Report."</ref>
Additionally, the site is associated with the military expeditions of {{ill|Prince Maroko|ja|麻呂子親王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Maroko|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 当麻皇子}}, a son of [[:en:Emperor Yōmei|Emperor Yōmei]], who is said to have prayed here before subduing rebellious forces in Ōeyama.
=== Later History === Komushi Shrine is listed alongside Ōmushi Shrine as a {{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōjin Taisha|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} in the [[:en:Engishiki|Engishiki]]. The shrine's relocation to its current site, along with Ōmushi Shrine, likely occurred in the early [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], though the exact circumstances are unclear.<ref>Kayachō History. A remnant of the former site, known as "Mitarashi Pond," is said to exist.</ref>
In 1873, the shrine was classified as a [[Village Shrine]] under [[Toyooka Prefecture]]. The current [[:en:Honden|Main Hall]] was reconstructed starting in 1873 and completed in 1877. During this time, it absorbed Hirogaki Shrine and Kijimi Shrine, which were nearby.
== Rituals == The shrine's parishioners include six districts of Atsue: Omoriya, Onoue, Monde, Tanigaki, Osa, and Homura. Rituals are conducted jointly with Ōmushi Shrine, including performances of kagura (sacred dances) and sword dances. These festivities are part of the annual "Kayadani Festival," held on the last weekend of April.
== Structures == The [[:en:Honden|Main Hall]] was rebuilt in 1877 in the [[:en:Shinmei-zukuri|Shinmei-zukuri]] style. Other structures include the [[:en:Haiden (Shinto)|Worship Hall]], a {{ill|Stage Pavilion|ja|舞殿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Stage Pavilion|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and several auxiliary shrines, such as Wakamiya Shrine, Komori Shrine, and Nekomiya Shrine.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Kayachō Compilation Committee, ''Kayachō History,'' Kayachō Town Hall, 1974 * Kayachō Compilation Committee, ''Kayachō History: Materials Edition,'' Volumes 1 and 2, Yosano Town Hall, 2007-2008 * Shikinai Shrine Research Society, ''Shikinai Shrine Investigation Report,'' Volume 18: San'in Region 1, Kōgakkan University Press, 1984 * Tanikawa Ken'ichi, ed., ''Japanese Gods and Sacred Sites,'' Vol. 7: San'in Region, Hakusuisha, 2000
== External Links == * [http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/376001.html Komushi Shrine] (Kokugakuin University, "Shinto and Shrine Historical Resources")

'''Komushi Shrine''' ({{ill|Komushi Shrine|ja|小虫神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Komushi Shrine|qq=}}) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[Atsue]], [[Yosano Town]], [[Yosa District]], [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]]. It is a designated {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōjin Taisha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 名神大社}}) and Ranked as a [[Village Shrine]] under the [[Modern typeing System]]. The shrine is situated on an eastern plateau of the Kayadani area along the midstream of the {{ill|Nodagawa River (Kyoto)|ja|野田川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nodagawa River (Kyoto)|1=Nodagawa River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11645972}}, at the western base of the Ōeyama mountain range. Kayadani is known for its concentration of ancient Kofuns and archaeological sites, such as the nationally designated [[Hiruko-yama Kofun]] and the [[Sakuyama Kofun (Yosano Town)|Sakuyama Kofun]], making it a key area in the ancient [[:en:Tango Province|Tango Province]].
== Deities == * {{ill|Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}} (The shrine records from 1883 note an alternate tradition enshrining "Wakayama-kui no Mikoto." <ref>Included in Volume 1 of the "Kayachō History: Materials Edition."</ref>) * {{ill|Kagutsuchi no Mikoto|ja|火産霊命|1=Kagu-tsuchi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kagutsuchi no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Kagu-tsuchi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to カグツチ}} (from Hirogaki Shrine, merged in 1877) * {{ill|Ōyamatsumi no Mikoto|ja|大山祇命|1=Ōyamatsumi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamatsumi no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamatsumi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to オオヤマツミ}} (from Kijimi Shrine, merged simultaneously with Hirogaki Shrine)
The primary deity, {{ill|Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}} (or Wakayama-kui no Mikoto), is regarded as the god responsible for the construction of Yosa District and the guardian deity of hot springs.<ref>Kayachō History. Regarding the current location of "Atsue," the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] scholar {{ill|Yoshida Tōgo|ja|吉田東伍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshida Tōgo|1=Tōgo Yoshida|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11413910}} suggested the name originated from the ancient presence of hot springs, although no springs remain as of the Meiji era.</ref>
== History == === Founding Tradition === The shrine was originally located in Ikegamaru, a site on the slopes of the Ōeyama mountain range, alongside the nearby {{ill|Ōmushi Shrine (Yosano Town)|ja|大虫神社 (与謝野町)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmushi Shrine (Yosano Town)|1=Omushi Shrine (Yosano)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11438782|lt=Ōmushi Shrine (Yosano Town)}}. In ancient times, {{ill|Ōkuninushi no Mikoto|ja|大国主命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主|1=Ōkuninushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q276944}}, enshrined at Ōmushi Shrine, was said to reside in the area with {{ill|Numagawa-hime|ja|沼河姫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Numagawa-hime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 沼河比売|1=Nunakawa hime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11142832}}. According to legend, {{ill|Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少彦名命|1=Sukunabikona|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}} cured the poisoned Numagawa-hime, who had fallen ill due to the malice of a demon named Tsuchi-oni. This act gave rise to the worship of "Ōmushi" and "Komushi" as protector deities.<ref name="InvestigationReport">"Shikinai Shrine Investigation Report."</ref>
Additionally, the site is associated with the military expeditions of {{ill|Prince Maroko|ja|麻呂子親王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Maroko|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 当麻皇子}}, a son of [[:en:Emperor Yōmei|Emperor Yōmei]], who is said to have prayed here before subduing rebellious forces in Ōeyama.
=== Later History === Komushi Shrine is listed alongside Ōmushi Shrine as a {{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōjin Taisha|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} in the [[:en:Engishiki|Engishiki]]. The shrine's relocation to its current site, along with Ōmushi Shrine, likely occurred in the early [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], though the exact circumstances are unclear.<ref>Kayachō History. A remnant of the former site, known as "Mitarashi Pond," is said to exist.</ref>
In 1873, the shrine was classified as a [[Village Shrine]] under [[Toyooka Prefecture]]. The current [[:en:Honden|Main Hall]] was reconstructed starting in 1873 and completed in 1877. During this time, it absorbed Hirogaki Shrine and Kijimi Shrine, which were nearby.
== Rituals == The shrine's parishioners include six districts of Atsue: Omoriya, Onoue, Monde, Tanigaki, Osa, and Homura. Rituals are conducted jointly with Ōmushi Shrine, including performances of kagura (sacred dances) and sword dances. These festivities are part of the annual "Kayadani Festival," held on the last weekend of April.
== Structures == The [[:en:Honden|Main Hall]] was rebuilt in 1877 in the [[:en:Shinmei-zukuri|Shinmei-zukuri]] style. Other structures include the [[:en:Haiden (Shinto)|Worship Hall]], a {{ill|Stage Pavilion|ja|舞殿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Stage Pavilion|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and several auxiliary shrines, such as Wakamiya Shrine, Komori Shrine, and Nekomiya Shrine.

=== Later History === Komushi Shrine is listed alongside Ōmushi Shrine as a {{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōjin Taisha|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} in the [[:en:Engishiki|Engishiki]]. The shrine's relocation to its current site, along with Ōmushi Shrine, likely occurred in the early [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], though the exact circumstances are unclear.<ref>Kayachō History. A remnant of the former site, known as "Mitarashi Pond," is said to exist.</ref>
In 1873, the shrine was classified as a [[Village Shrine]] under [[Toyooka Prefecture]]. The current [[:en:Honden|Main Hall]] was reconstructed starting in 1873 and completed in 1877. During this time, it absorbed Hirogaki Shrine and Kijimi Shrine, which were nearby.
== Rituals == The shrine's parishioners include six districts of Atsue: Omoriya, Onoue, Monde, Tanigaki, Osa, and Homura. Rituals are conducted jointly with Ōmushi Shrine, including performances of kagura (sacred dances) and sword dances. These festivities are part of the annual "Kayadani Festival," held on the last weekend of April.
== Structures == The [[:en:Honden|Main Hall]] was rebuilt in 1877 in the [[:en:Shinmei-zukuri|Shinmei-zukuri]] style. Other structures include the [[:en:Haiden (Shinto)|Worship Hall]], a {{ill|Stage Pavilion|ja|舞殿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Stage Pavilion|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and several auxiliary shrines, such as Wakamiya Shrine, Komori Shrine, and Nekomiya Shrine.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Kayachō Compilation Committee, ''Kayachō History,'' Kayachō Town Hall, 1974 * Kayachō Compilation Committee, ''Kayachō History: Materials Edition,'' Volumes 1 and 2, Yosano Town Hall, 2007-2008 * Shikinai Shrine Research Society, ''Shikinai Shrine Investigation Report,'' Volume 18: San'in Region 1, Kōgakkan University Press, 1984 * Tanikawa Ken'ichi, ed., ''Japanese Gods and Sacred Sites,'' Vol. 7: San'in Region, Hakusuisha, 2000
== External Links == * [http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/376001.html Komushi Shrine] (Kokugakuin University, "Shinto and Shrine Historical Resources")
